segments.title: Third Queen Elizabeth carrier procurement answer
segments.summary: A viewer asks whether keeping a third carrier in 2000 could have led to an Invincible F-35B conversion; the answer argues delay costs could instead have funded another Queen Elizabeth-class carrier.
segments.body: This live Q&A segment captures a clean audience prompt about British carrier choices. Dr. Clarke answers that the money spent delaying Queen Elizabeth-class construction, once research and development costs are separated, was large enough to represent the cost of an additional carrier, so the stronger procurement point is not an Invincible conversion but the lost third Queen Elizabeth-class ship. Later live-Q&A review can add further questions from the rest of the stream.

evidence.note: The viewer asks whether a 2000 decision to keep a third carrier could have produced an Invincible conversion for F-35B operations.
The answer argues that delay payments and yard costs could have funded a third Queen Elizabeth-class carrier, leaving Britain with the cost equivalent of more carriers than it received.

segments.question: If the UK in 2000 kept the third carrier, could one of the Invincibles have been refitted as an F-35B carrier?
segments.answerShort: Dr. Clarke argues the larger issue is that delaying the Queen Elizabeth-class build consumed enough money to have paid for an additional Queen Elizabeth-class carrier, rather than making an Invincible conversion the key missed option.
